text: A sixty-two year old man with juvenile polyposis presented with chronic anemia requiring weekly iron infusions. He had previously undergone a laparoscopic great hemicolectomy for a bleeding polyp. At this time, however, endoscopic workup determined that multiple gastric polyps were responsible for the bleeding, with other GI sources ruled out. Laparoscopic total gastrectomy was recommended.

The surgical team performs an intraoperative upper endoscopy, revealing a normal gastroesophageal junction and duodenum, but a stomach filled with numerous sessile and pedunculated polyps. Ports are placed in the upper abdomen after insufflation. The stomach appears large and bulky with a transverse staple line across the antrum. The transverse colon is reflected cephalad, and the ligament of Treitz is identified. A 30 cm biliopancreatic limb is created using a 2.5 mm stapler, and the mesentery is divided with a harmonic device. A 45 cm Roux limb is measured to reach the hiatus without tension, and a stapled jejuno-jejunostomy is created. Brolin anti-kinking and crotch stitches are placed, and the small bowel mesenteric defect is closed with a running silk stitch.

The surgeon uses a Nathanson liver retractor to elevate the left lateral segment of the liver. The short gastric vessels are divided, exposing the left crus. The gastrohepatic ligament and phrenoesophageal membrane are opened to mobilize the esophagus. An accessory left hepatic artery is transected with a vascular staple load. The peritoneum over the right crus is opened, and the esophagus is mobilized circumferentially. The left gastric artery and coronary vein are ligated with a vascular staple load.

The surgeon frees the greater curvature of the stomach distally toward the pylorus, identifying, clipping, and dividing the gastroboploic vessels. The pyloric sphincter is precisely located, and the antrum is reflected upward. Dense posterior adhesions to the pancreas are divided, and the duodenum is transected just distal to the pylorus using a 3.5 mm endoscopic stapler. The stomach is now completely free and mobilized.

The surgeon places two traction sutures bilaterally in the esophagus several centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The esophagus is transected at the gastroesophageal junction using a 3.5 mm stapler. An oral device is used to pass a 25 mm EEA anvil transorally through a small esophagotomy made just posterior to the staple line.

The Roux limb is brought up to the hiatus in an anticholic fashion without tension. The left subcostal port is enlarged, and the EEA stapler is inserted through an enterotomy in the Roux limb, with the spike emerging through its anti-mesenteric surface. The spike is joined to the anvil in the distal esophagus, creating an end-to-side esophagojejunal anastomosis. Two complete donuts are retrieved, and the enterotomy site on the Roux limb is excised. Several reinforcing Lembert sutures are placed from the jejunum to the esophagus.

The surgeon occludes the roux limb with a bowel clamp and performs an air leak test, which is negative. The jejunum is marked 15 centimeters distal to the enteroenterostomy for the placement of a feeding jejunostomy tube. The two left subcostal port sites are connected to provide space for extracting the bulky specimen, and a Witzel jejunostomy is created through this incision.
